content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 23, 1990

Honorable Bev Hansen
Assemblywoman Eighth District
State Capitol
P.O. Box 942849
Sacramento, CA 94249-0001

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-424

Dear Assemblywoman Hansen:

You have requested advice concerning application of provi-
sions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1 with respect to
your current living arrangements.

QUESTION

Can you continue your current living arrangements, which
involve using an extra room in the home of long time family
friends, now that one of the family members has become a
registered lobbyist?

CONCLUSION

Your current living arrangement may be continued without
violating the Act provided that you provide consideration of equal
or greater value in exchange for your lodging.

FACTS

According to your letter, you have been using an extra room
in the Sacramento home of close family friends as living quarters
for two or three nights a week while the Legislature is in
session. The couple is present during the time you use the home.
You have been close friends with the couple for over ten years,
long before your election to the Assembly.

___________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-90-424
Page 2


You have not been paying direct rent for the use of the room.
However, you have paid for incidental expenses such as
babysitter's car repairs, groceries, and other expenses related to
the couple's young child.

One of the couple has now registered as a lobbyist, and you
are concerned about the application of the Act to your situation.

ANALYSIS

As a threshold matter, our advice is limited to application
of the Act to you as an elected officer. We cannot give third
party advice regarding the application of the Act to your friend
who is now a lobbyist. (Regulation 18329(c)(4)(C), copy
enclosed.) Furthermore, our advice is prospective only; we do not
advise regarding past conduct. (Regulation 18329(c)(4)(A).)

The Act prohibits an elected state officer from receiving a
gift from a lobbyist aggregating more than $10 in a calendar
month. (Sections 86201 - 86204.) As a member of the Assembly,
you are an elected state officer. (Section 82024.) The Act
defines a gift to include any payment to the extent that
consideration of equal or greater value is not received. (Section
82028.) "Payment" includes the rendering of money, property,
services or anything else of value. (Section 82044.) This would
apply to the rendering of lodging.

An exception to the gift limitation applicable to lobbyists
has been recognized with respect to "home hospitality" as follows:

(a) The cost of providing hospitality
involving food, beverage or occasional lodging at
the home of a lobbyist is a gift within the meaning
of Section 86203 and is reportable under the
provisions of Section 86113 only if:

(1) Any part of the cost of such
hospitality is paid for by the lobbyist's
employer or lobbying firm directly; or

(2) The lobbyist is reimbursed by his or
her lobbyist employer or lobbying firm for any
part of the cost of such hospitality; or

(3) The lobbyist deducts any part of the
cost of such hospitality as a business expense
on any tax return, either State or Federal; or

(4) There is an understanding between
the lobbyist and his or her lobbyist employer
or lobbying firm that the amount of
compensation received by the lobbyist includes
a portion to be utilized by the lobbyist to

Our File No. A-90-424
Page 3

provide gifts of hospitality in the lobbyist's
home.

(b) In determining the applicability of
subsections (a)(1) through (a)(4) above, the cost
of providing hospitality does not include any part
of the value of rental of the home of the lobbyist,
nor does it include any depreciation on the
premises where the hospitality is extended.

Regulation 18630, copy
enclosed.

Regulation 18630 provides an exception for "occasional lodg-
ing." Exceptions should be narrowly construed. (Estate of
Banerjee (1978) 21 Cal.3d 527,540.) We interpret the exception
for occasional lodging to apply to infrequent lodging and not to
the type of regular, ongoing arrangement that you describe.
Therefore, we do not believe that the exception for occasional
home hospitality would apply to your situation.²

Therefore, in order to avoid the application of the $10 gift
limitation, you must provide consideration of equal or greater
value in exchange for the lodging provided. You indicate in your
letter that you have been providing payments for certain
incidental expenses as a form of indirect rent. We would advise,
in order to avoid a gift, that you formalize your living arrange-
ment and pay rent directly. The rent may be in cash or in kind,
but should be agreed upon consideration that is greater or equal
to the fair rental value of the lodging provided.

_________________________

2  We have recognized a limited exception in the case of certain
bona fide close dating relationships. (Javor Advice Letter, No.
78-088; Shea Advice Letter, No. A-84-085; Murphy Advice Letter,
No. A-87-313; Roff Advice Letter, No. A-89-106; Davidge Advice
Letter, No. A-89-134, copies enclosed.) Where the situation does
not involve a lobbyist, we have also recognized a limited excep-
tion for neighborly acts involving expenditures of less than $50.
(In re Corr. (1975) 1 FPPC Ops 153; Roberti Advice Letter, No. A-
85-002, copies enclosed.) Even if these exceptions applied to the
close family relationship you describe, neither would permit the
exception for your lodging arrangement. The exception for
neighborly acts has been limited to circumstances involving
expenditures of $50 or less which do not involve lobbyists.
(Roberti Advice letter, supra.) The exception for close dating
relationships has been applied only to "truly personal gifts, such
as gifts of entertainment, meals, personal property or expenses
involved in recreational travel...." (Shea Advice Letter, supra.)

Our File No. A-90-424
Page 4


     I trust that this letter provides you with the necessary
information concerning the question you have raised. If you have
any further questions, you can contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]

                                        By: Margaret W. Ellison
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

SH:MWE:aa

Enclosures

sections.question: Can you continue your current living arrangements, which involve using an extra room in the home of long time family friends, now that one of the family members has become a registered lobbyist?
sections.conclusion: Your current living arrangement may be continued without violating the Act provided that you provide consideration of equal or greater value in exchange for your lodging.

The couple is present during the time you use the home. You have been close friends with the couple for over ten years, long before your election to the Assembly.

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

You have not been paying direct rent for the use of the room. However, you have paid for incidental expenses such as babysitter's car repairs, groceries, and other expenses related to the couple's young child.

One of the couple has now registered as a lobbyist, and you are concerned about the application of the Act to your situation.
sections.analysis:
As a threshold matter, our advice is limited to application of the Act to you as an elected officer. We cannot give third party advice regarding the application of the Act to your friend who is now a lobbyist. (Regulation 18329(c)(4)(C), copy enclosed.) Furthermore, our advice is prospective only; we do not advise regarding past conduct. (Regulation 18329(c)(4)(A).)

The Act prohibits an elected state officer from receiving a gift from a lobbyist aggregating more than $10 in a calendar month. (Sections 86201 - 86204.) As a member of the Assembly, you are an elected state officer. (Section 82024.) The Act defines a gift to include any payment to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received. (Section 82028.) "Payment" includes the rendering of money, property, services or anything else of value. (Section 82044.) This would apply to the rendering of lodging.

An exception to the gift limitation applicable to lobbyists has been recognized with respect to "home hospitality" as follows:

(a) The cost of providing hospitality involving food, beverage or occasional lodging at the home of a lobbyist is a gift within the meaning of Section 86203 and is reportable under the provisions of Section 86113 only if:

    (1) Any part of the cost of such hospitality is paid for by the lobbyist's employer or lobbying firm directly; or

    (2) The lobbyist is reimbursed by his or her lobbyist employer or lobbying firm for any part of the cost of such hospitality; or

    (3) The lobbyist deducts any part of the cost of such hospitality as a business expense on any tax return, either State or Federal; or

    (4) There is an understanding between the lobbyist and his or her lobbyist employer or lobbying firm that the amount of compensation received by the lobbyist includes a portion to be utilized by the lobbyist to

provide gifts of hospitality in the lobbyist's home.

(b) In determining the applicability of subsections (a)(1) through (a)(4) above, the cost of providing hospitality does not include any part of the value of rental of the home of the lobbyist, nor does it include any depreciation on the premises where the hospitality is extended.

Regulation 18630, copy enclosed.

Regulation 18630 provides an exception for "occasional lodging." Exceptions should be narrowly construed. (Estate of Banerjee (1978) 21 Cal.3d 527,540.) We interpret the exception for occasional lodging to apply to infrequent lodging and not to the type of regular, ongoing arrangement that you describe. Therefore, we do not believe that the exception for occasional home hospitality would apply to your situation.2

Therefore, in order to avoid the application of the $10 gift limitation, you must provide consideration of equal or greater value in exchange for the lodging provided. You indicate in your letter that you have been providing payments for certain incidental expenses as a form of indirect rent. We would advise, in order to avoid a gift, that you formalize your living arrangement and pay rent directly. The rent may be in cash or in kind, but should be agreed upon consideration that is greater or equal to the fair rental value of the lodging provided.

2 We have recognized a limited exception in the case of certain bona fide close dating relationships. (Javor Advice Letter, No. 78-088; Shea Advice Letter, No. A-84-085; Murphy Advice Letter, No. A-87-313; Roff Advice Letter, No. A-89-106; Davidge Advice Letter, No. A-89-134, copies enclosed.) Where the situation does not involve a lobbyist, we have also recognized a limited exception for neighborly acts involving expenditures of less than $50. (In re Cory (1975) 1 FPPC Ops 153; Roberti Advice Letter, No. A-85-002, copies enclosed.) Even if these exceptions applied to the close family relationship you describe, neither would permit the exception for your lodging arrangement. The exception for neighborly acts has been limited to circumstances involving expenditures of $50 or less and which do not involve lobbyists. (Roberti Advice Letter, supra.) The exception for close dating relationships has been applied only to "truly personal gifts, such as gifts of entertainment, meals, personal property or expenses involved in recreational travel...." (Shea Advice Letter, supra.)

I trust that this letter provides you with the necessary information concerning the question you have raised. If you have any further questions, you can contact me at (916) 322-5901.

QUESTION: Can you continue your current living arrangements, which involve using an extra room in the home of long time family friends, now that one of the family members has become a registered lobbyist?

CONCLUSION: Your current living arrangement may be continued without violating the Act provided that you provide consideration of equal or greater value in exchange for your lodging.
